worth_it_explanation: Programmatic deals carry negotiated rates that usually clear above the open market, so a deal that stalls or one that is priced against the open auction the wrong way trades premium revenue for whatever the open market happens to pay. This check reviews deal health and deal pricing that a publisher already controls in PubMatic, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and each finding points to a concrete deal the publisher can act on.
intro: Programmatic deals are negotiated agreements that route specific demand to a publisher's inventory at terms set in advance. Private Marketplace (PMP) deals run an invitation-only auction at or above a negotiated floor, and Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deals commit a fixed price and a fixed volume. Both usually clear above the open market, which is why they exist. These checks confirm that the deals a publisher has set up in PubMatic are actually delivering, with deal IDs active and spending rather than stalled, and that deal floors and priorities are set so the deals hold their negotiated value instead of being undercut by or undercutting the open auction.

audit_checks.title: Deal health is not monitored, so stalled or underdelivering deals go unnoticed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A negotiated deal that stops spending, or never starts, takes premium demand off the table while the inventory clears at the open-market price instead. Without a regular read of deal status and spend, a stalled deal ID, a paused buyer, or a targeting mismatch can sit unresolved for weeks, so the revenue the deal was meant to bring is lost for that whole window.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal is only worth what it actually delivers. Setting up a Private Marketplace (PMP) or Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal in PubMatic creates the deal ID and the terms, but the deal still has to fire, win, and spend for the negotiated rate to reach the publisher. A deal can be live in the platform and contributing nothing because the buyer paused their campaign, the deal ID was never wired into the buyer's seat correctly, or the targeting on either side no longer matches the inventory the deal was meant to reach.

When a deal stalls, the inventory does not go dark. It falls back to the open auction and clears at whatever the open market pays, which is usually below the negotiated deal rate. That makes a stalled deal easy to miss because the slots still fill and revenue still flows, just at a lower price than the deal was meant to secure. The gap between what the deal should have brought and what the open market paid is the cost, and it stays invisible without a read of deal-level delivery.

Monitoring deal health means tracking each active deal ID against what it was set up to do. A few signals show whether a deal is delivering or stalled:
  • Impressions and spend per deal ID, to confirm the deal is firing and winning rather than sitting idle
  • Bid rate and win rate for the deal, to separate a deal that is bidding but losing from one that is not bidding at all
  • Pacing against the negotiated volume for a Programmatic Guaranteed deal, to catch underdelivery before the term ends
You may want to confirm that someone reviews deal delivery on a regular cadence rather than assuming a configured deal is a delivering deal. One approach is to read impressions, spend, and win rate per deal ID on a set schedule, flag any active deal showing little or no delivery, and reconcile it with the buyer before the revenue window closes.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, deals are often negotiated around specific sections or sponsorship-adjacent placements that carry a premium, so a stalled deal there means the premium inventory falls back to the open auction at a lower rate. You may want to monitor deal delivery by the placements each deal targets, so a deal that stops spending on a premium section surfaces quickly rather than hiding inside a site-wide total.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, deals may target product or category pages where the audience is closer to a purchase and the negotiated rate reflects that intent. You may want to track deal delivery on the commercial inventory specifically because a stalled deal there hands higher-intent impressions to the open market at a price that does not reflect their value.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server Deals for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration and reporting documentation for deal monitoring is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each active deal ID against its impressions, spend, win rate, and pacing to see which deals are delivering and which are stalled, and check whether deal health is reviewed on a set cadence.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the deals section for the site and list every active deal ID with its type (PMP or Programmatic Guaranteed), buyer, and negotiated terms.
For each deal, record impressions and spend over a recent representative period to see whether the deal is firing and winning or sitting idle.
Note any active deal showing little or no delivery, and flag whether a regular review of deal health exists or whether deals are set up and left unchecked.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by deal ID with impressions, spend, bid rate, and win rate over a representative period.
Separate deals that are bidding but losing from deals that are not bidding at all because the cause and the fix differ.
For each Programmatic Guaranteed deal, compare delivered impressions against the negotiated volume and the pacing the term requires, and note any deal running behind.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active deal ID alongside its impressions, spend, win rate, and, for Programmatic Guaranteed deals, its pacing against the negotiated volume, and notes whether deal health is reviewed on a regular cadence. A setup where deals are monitored on a set schedule and any stalled or underdelivering deal is flagged and reconciled passes. A setup where active deals show little or no delivery with no review process in place is a fail recorded with the deal IDs affected and the delivery gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active deal ID with its impressions, spend, win rate, and pacing above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor the health of its programmatic deals, so stalled or underdelivering deal IDs can sit unnoticed. A deal that stops spending hands its inventory back to the open auction at a lower price than the negotiated rate, and because the slots still fill, the gap stays invisible without a deal-level read. {{voice_recommend}} reading impressions, spend, and win rate per deal ID on a set cadence, checking pacing against the negotiated volume for each Programmatic Guaranteed deal, and reconciling any deal showing little delivery with the buyer before the revenue window closes. After the review, keep the cadence so a stalled deal surfaces while there is still time to recover it.

audit_checks.title: Deal floors and priorities are not aligned with the open market
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A deal floor set below the open-market clearing price lets the deal win impressions for less than the open auction would have paid, while a deal priority that does not favor the negotiated demand lets the open market outrank a deal the publisher meant to protect. Either misalignment trades the premium a deal was negotiated to capture for whatever the open auction returns, on the specific inventory the deal covers.

educate.base:
A programmatic deal and the open auction compete for the same impressions, so the floor and the priority a publisher sets on the deal decide which one wins and at what price. A Private Marketplace (PMP) deal carries a negotiated floor that is meant to clear above the open market because that premium is the reason the deal exists. A Programmatic Guaranteed (PG) deal carries a fixed price and an expectation that it takes precedence over open-auction demand. When the floors and priorities behind those deals do not line up with the open market, the deal stops doing what it was negotiated to do.

The misalignment runs in two directions. A deal floor set below the price the open market already clears at means the deal can win an impression for less than the open auction would have paid, so the deal undercuts the publisher's own open-market revenue rather than adding to it. The opposite case also costs money. A deal priority that does not favor the negotiated demand lets a strong open-market bid outrank a deal the publisher meant to protect, so the guaranteed or premium demand loses impressions it was supposed to receive and the deal underdelivers.

Because PubMatic's wrapper is built on Prebid, the deal mechanics follow Prebid's deal handling, where a deal can be ranked ahead of non-deal demand when the publisher chooses to prefer deals, and deal line items carry their own priority and pricing. Prebid's own guidance describes setting deal line items at a priority above open-market bids and populating deal targeting so the deal is recognized and ranked. The audit question is whether each deal's floor sits in the right relationship to the open-market clearing price for the inventory it covers, and whether its priority favors the negotiated demand as intended.

A few signs point to deal floors and priorities that are not aligned:
  • A deal floor sits below the average open-market clearing price for the same inventory, so the deal can win for less than the open auction would have paid
  • Deal line items are not prioritized above open-market demand, so a strong open bid can outrank a deal meant to take precedence
  • No comparison exists between deal clearing prices and open-market clearing prices on the inventory each deal covers
You may want to confirm that each deal's floor and priority hold the deal in the right relationship to the open market. One approach is to compare deal clearing prices against open-market clearing prices on the same inventory, raise any deal floor that sits below the open market, and set deal priorities so the negotiated demand ranks where the agreement intends.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium sections often carry both a negotiated deal and strong open-market demand, so a deal floor set below what the section already clears at lets the deal undercut the open auction on the publisher's best inventory. You may want to compare deal floors against open-market clearing prices section by section, so the deal floor protects the premium rather than sitting beneath it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can draw competitive open-market bids because of the purchase intent, so a deal on that inventory needs a floor and a priority that hold against strong open demand. You may want to confirm that deals on commercial pages are floored above the open-market clearing price there and prioritized so the negotiated demand is not outranked by an open bid on the highest-intent inventory.

learn_more.source_title: Prebid Server Deals for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for deal floors and priorities is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each deal's floor and priority against the open-market clearing price for the inventory it covers, and check whether the deal ranks where the agreement intends.

pubmatic_console.label: PubMatic publisher console
steps: Open the deals section and list each active deal ID with its negotiated floor, its type (PMP or Programmatic Guaranteed), and the inventory it targets.
For each deal, record the priority or ranking the deal carries relative to open-market demand.
Note whether any deal floor or priority appears to have been set without reference to the open-market price on the same inventory.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic reporting
steps: Build a report comparing deal clearing prices against open-market clearing prices for the same ad units or sections each deal covers.
Flag any deal whose clearing price sits at or below the open-market price, which points to a deal floor set too low to add value over the open auction.
Note any case where a deal underdelivers while strong open-market demand exists on the same inventory, which points to a priority that does not favor the deal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each deal's floor and priority alongside the open-market clearing price for the inventory it covers. Deals floored above the open-market clearing price and prioritized so the negotiated demand ranks where the agreement intends pass. A deal floored at or below the open market, or one whose priority lets open-market demand outrank it against the agreement, is a fail recorded with the deal IDs and the inventory where the floor or priority diverges from the open market.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each deal's floor and priority alongside the open-market clearing price for the same inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} sets deal floors and priorities without aligning them to the open market, so the negotiated deals do not hold the value they were meant to capture. A deal floored below the open-market clearing price can win impressions for less than the open auction would have paid, while a deal priority that does not favor the negotiated demand lets a strong open bid outrank a deal that was meant to take precedence. {{voice_recommend}} comparing deal clearing prices against open-market clearing prices on the same inventory, raising any deal floor that sits below the open market, and setting deal priorities so the negotiated demand ranks where the agreement intends. After the change, recheck deal delivery and clearing price to confirm the deals hold their premium against the open auction.
